jokes.text: Abbi wakes to a rat in her apartment and screams 'Rat Bastard!'
jokes.setup: Cold open, morning routine implied. Sudden chaos of discovering a rat.
jokes.punchline: 'Rat Bastard!' as a fully committed curse at a rat — the specificity and fury of the insult directed at a rodent.
jokes.explanation: Works as a title drop and character establishing moment — Abbi's complete emotional commitment to insulting a rat is perfectly on-brand. 'Rat Bastard' becomes the episode's running name for the rat, giving it episode-long payoff value.

jokes.text: Abbi screams at the rat: 'Get out of here! You're ruining my life!'
jokes.setup: The rat is a nuisance pest in her apartment.
jokes.punchline: The escalation from 'get out' to 'you're ruining my LIFE' — treating an insect-level inconvenience as an existential catastrophe.
jokes.explanation: Serviceable escalation joke. The hyperbole is mild but characterizes Abbi's tendency toward dramatic reaction. Slightly undermined by its proximity to the stronger 'Rat Bastard' cold open.

jokes.text: Abbi says 'This is some of my best sandwich work to date' while the rat chaos is ongoing.
jokes.setup: Abbi is dealing with a rat infestation in her apartment.
jokes.punchline: She pivots completely to self-congratulatory sandwich assessment — total tonal whiplash that reveals her priorities.
jokes.explanation: This is a genuinely sharp character joke. In the middle of vermin panic, Abbi stops to evaluate her sandwich with the seriousness of a food critic. 'To date' implies an ongoing body of sandwich work being tracked and assessed. Economy is excellent — four words do a lot of character work. Reveals Abbi's split-focus neuroticism hilariously.

jokes.text: Ilana says 'Rata hijueputa' (Colombian Spanish: 'rat son of a bitch') then immediately adds 'Kinda sexy.'
jokes.setup: Both women are horrified by the rat.
jokes.punchline: Ilana curses at the rat in Spanish, then undermines her own disgust by finding it 'kinda sexy' — a non-sequitur that reveals her bizarre attraction filter.
jokes.explanation: This is a peak Ilana moment — multilingual disgust immediately pivoting to arousal is so specific to her character it couldn't be anyone else's line. The Spanish profanity grounds her in Jaimé's cultural influence, then 'Kinda sexy' demolishes any dignity. High character integration score is fully justified. Almost certainly improvised or improv-adjacent.

jokes.text: Ilana yells 'Weed thief!' at the rat (implied the rat ate/stole her weed).
jokes.setup: The rat has been causing destruction.
jokes.punchline: Among all possible grievances, Ilana's most personal offense is the rat stealing her weed — a perfectly Ilana-coded priority.
jokes.explanation: Two words, maximum character specificity. Only Ilana would classify a rat as a 'weed thief' — the word 'thief' applies a moral judgment that humanizes both the rat and Ilana's relationship to her stash. Excellent economy.

jokes.text: Exterminator says rat is 'gone to a better place.' Abbi: 'Oh, you mean, like the heaven.' Exterminator: 'Oh, no. His dead carcass is probably rotting in the ceiling.'
jokes.setup: Exterminator uses comforting language suggesting the rat is at peace.
jokes.punchline: The brutal clarification — not heaven, just decomposing inside the wall. Abbi's 'like the heaven' adds an extra naive layer before the gut-punch.
jokes.explanation: Strong three-part structure: comforting euphemism → Abbi's sweet misreading ('like the heaven' is a great touch, the 'like' implies she barely believes it herself) → exterminator's stone-cold correction. The deadpan delivery of 'probably rotting in the ceiling' from a professional is the engine. Well-constructed.

jokes.text: Exterminator says '$400.' Abbi and Ilana react with shock, then man says 'I'm gonna have to sell my sperm again.'
jokes.setup: The women are already strapped for cash.
jokes.punchline: 'I'm gonna have to sell my sperm again' — the 'again' is the key word, implying this is a recurring financial crisis strategy, and one he's reluctant to return to.
jokes.explanation: 'Again' does all the heavy lifting — this single word transforms a one-off desperation joke into a window into a whole character history. The resigned 'uh...' trailing off adds to the portrait. Solid economy.

jokes.text: Gift basket labeled 'Larry and David' arrives — the name clearly evoking Larry David of Curb Your Enthusiasm.
jokes.setup: A mysterious gift basket arrives addressed 'Larry and David.'
jokes.punchline: The name is the setup for a long-running misunderstanding — the audience immediately clocks Larry David, while the characters will interpret it differently. Also a Comedy Central meta-wink.
jokes.explanation: Smart planted joke with long fuse — the Larry David reference is immediately legible to the audience but remains ambiguous to the characters. Rewatch value because the eventual reveal pays this off. Good structural planting.

jokes.text: Ilana asks Abbi about her nice dress: 'Hot date later? Is he black?'
jokes.setup: Ilana compliments Abbi's dress and asks about a hot date.
jokes.punchline: The immediate, non-sequitur follow-up 'Is he black?' — Ilana's fetishizing enthusiasm firing faster than her social filter.
jokes.explanation: Quintessential Ilana joke — her racial fetishization is a recurring character trait handled with ironic self-awareness by the show. The joke works because it's so specifically Ilana, the instant pivot from compliment to 'Is he black?' lands as character revelation rather than cheap shock. Abbi's bewildered 'What?' is the perfect reaction beat.

jokes.text: Ilana introduces 'kom-booze-cha' — her word for alcoholic kombucha — then immediately says 'Don't steal that, though. I came up with it.'
jokes.setup: Ilana offers Abbi a high-alcohol kombucha.
jokes.punchline: The portmanteau 'kom-booze-cha' is immediately undercut by Ilana's territorial IP protection of a pun anyone might make.
jokes.explanation: The pun is decent, but the joke is really 'don't steal that though' — Ilana's earnest protectiveness over an obvious wordplay reveal she considers herself an original thinker. That self-delusion is funnier than the pun. Good structure: present the thing, then immediately do the wrong thing about it.

jokes.text: Ilana excitedly catalogs the gift basket: 'Olive oil? Sea salt crackers? Baklava?! This is the most beautiful basket I've ever seen, and I grew up on Long Island. They had shivas for days, literally.'
jokes.setup: The gift basket arrives and Ilana starts listing contents.
jokes.punchline: Her Long Island Jewish background as the credentialing authority on beautiful gift baskets, specifically because of shiva culture — high-end snack credibility through mourning rituals.
jokes.explanation: Jewish shiva spread as the apex of gift basket culture is a specific and well-earned cultural reference. Ilana citing her Long Island Jewish upbringing to establish gift basket authority is perfectly absurd credentialism. 'Literally' at the end grounds the absurdity in sincerity.

jokes.text: Ilana concludes the basket is from her parents who haven't spoken to her since she came out, calling the olive oil 'an olive branch, literally' then adds 'Dur, there's olive tapenade in here. I mean, double dur.'
jokes.setup: The basket contains an olive-related product and Ilana is moved by her parents' gesture.
jokes.punchline: 'Olive branch, literally' is an intentional pun on the metaphor/physical object, which she then doubles down on with 'double dur' — gleeful self-satisfaction at her own wordplay in an emotionally significant moment.
jokes.explanation: The joke cuts both ways — it's funny because Ilana is genuinely moved and can't stop herself making a pun anyway, and 'double dur' implies she's competing with herself on wit level. The emotional context makes the interruption funnier.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Larry and David? That's two men, right?' — beginning to misread the gift basket as being from a gay couple, not her parents.
jokes.setup: The basket is from Larry and David (an upscale NY gift company, but also transparently Larry David).
jokes.punchline: Ilana starts constructing an entirely false narrative about whose gift this is, a misunderstanding that will power the whole episode.
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural setup — this single question plants the misunderstanding that drives the entire episode's B-plot. On rewatch, the layered misreading (Larry David the comedian/Larry David the company/gay couple) becomes richer. Clean, economical planting.

jokes.text: 'Sometimes we are so smart, Ilana, I am scared of what we are truly capable of.' — Abbi, after their obvious party idea.
jokes.setup: They come up with the basic idea of charging cover at their apartment party to recoup exterminator money.
jokes.punchline: Abbi treats this obvious, unoriginal idea like they've cracked cold fusion — exaggerated self-regard for minimal cleverness.
jokes.explanation: 'I am scared of what we are truly capable of' is a great line for its grandiosity applied to such a small, obvious plan. Works as both character comedy (their mutual self-inflation) and irony.

jokes.text: Phone call: Ilana describes the gift basket as 'insahn' — her idiosyncratic pronunciation/intensifier for 'insane.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is excitedly describing the basket to someone on the phone.
jokes.punchline: 'Insahn' — Ilana's stretched, affected pronunciation of 'insane' as her highest compliment.
jokes.explanation: A micro-joke — the stretch of 'insahn' is Ilana speaking her own dialect. Low setup but high character integration. Works as texture rather than a built joke.

jokes.text: Abbi on the phone: 'Bring friends... I, I don't have any, but you guys. That's cool.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is trying to fill a party.
jokes.punchline: Mid-pitch, she reveals she has no friends outside this group, then immediately self-soothes with 'That's cool.'
jokes.explanation: The rhythm is what makes it — the pause before 'That's cool' is a tiny beat of self-pity before she plows forward. 'That's cool' doing the work of an entire therapy session. Economical sad-funny character moment.

jokes.text: 'Caught Abbi red 'anded, creepin' on da Soulstice floor.' — A partygoer catching Abbi drinking alone in a closet.
jokes.setup: Abbi has been hiding to drink her embarrassing amount of booze.
jokes.punchline: She gets busted in a comedically dramatic way, but the dialect affectation ('red 'anded,' 'creepin'') is its own joke.
jokes.explanation: Minor joke, mostly a scene transition beat. The line itself isn't particularly crafted, but it sets up the next comedic sequence of Abbi being caught drinking alone.

jokes.text: Abbi explains she was drinking alone in a closet because it was her 'third and fourth bottle' and she didn't want anyone to see her 'consuming so many empty calories.'
jokes.setup: A man finds Abbi hiding and asks what she's doing.
jokes.punchline: The concern isn't the alcoholism quantity — it's the calorie shame. She's hiding three-to-four bottles deep but her stated reason is caloric optics.
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection of priority — the audience expects shame about drinking volume, but Abbi's explanation pivots to calorie anxiety. This layers real neurosis (body image) onto the comic behavior (hiding to drink) in a way that feels true. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Man says 'It's kind of spunky, right?' Abbi says 'That's what she said.' He says 'Come on, that was genuinely organic.' — A meta-commentary on 'That's what she said' jokes.
jokes.setup: A man uses 'spunky' and Abbi reflexively deploys a TWSS joke.
jokes.punchline: His defense 'Come on, that was genuinely organic' — arguing the quality and authenticity of a 'That's what she said' is absurd meta-comedy. The show is aware these jokes are cheap and calls it out while doing it.
jokes.explanation: This works on two levels: the expected cheap TWSS joke plus the meta-layer of him defending it. 'Genuinely organic' as a criterion for a TWSS — as if there's a craft standard — is a smart inversion. The show is mocking the joke-type while deploying it. High earned-vs-cheap because it earns its meta-awareness.

jokes.text: The closet-drinking man tries stand-up on Abbi: 'So I go into like a Starbucks... And the person's like, "What's your name?" And I'm like, "Mrs. Starbucks."'
jokes.setup: A man at a party decides this is the moment to try his material on Abbi.
jokes.punchline: 'Mrs. Starbucks' — an aggressively unfunny punchline delivered with complete confidence, making the cringe the actual joke.
jokes.explanation: The joke-within-a-joke structure: the internal 'joke' (Mrs. Starbucks) is intentionally terrible. The real joke is watching this man deliver something so weak with such commitment. His setup 'It's like a pretty big chain of coffee shops' / 'From Seattle! Get a clue, man!' adds to the cringe — he's doing crowd work on a one-person audience. High impact because bad jokes delivered with confidence are reliably funny to comedy-literate audiences.

jokes.text: The Starbucks 'Mrs. Starbucks' story — the stand-up bit at the party
jokes.setup: A comedian at the Summer Solstice Soulstice party is doing stand-up. He sets up an ordering story at Starbucks where the barista asks for a name
jokes.punchline: 'Mrs. Starbucks' — he told them his name was Mrs. Starbucks. The setup is painfully over-explained ('It's, like, a pretty big chain of coffee shops' / 'From Seattle! Get a clue, man!') and the punchline is extremely weak
jokes.explanation: This is intentionally terrible stand-up — the joke within the show is that this comedian is bad. The over-explanation of Starbucks ('pretty big chain... from Seattle!') and the limp punchline 'Mrs. Starbucks' are written to be cringe-inducing. The comedy is the badness of the performance, not the material itself. Scored low because as a joke it fails by design — the laugh is at the comedian, not with him.

jokes.text: Ilana answers phone mid-scene: 'Can you steal tampons, too?' and her friend starts bleeding — Ilana's immediate response is 'Dur, that's why I need the tampons.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is on the phone. It becomes apparent her friend is bleeding.
jokes.punchline: 'Dur, that's why I need the tampons' — delivered as if Abbi's alarm at the bleeding is the irrational response.
jokes.explanation: Ilana's complete lack of panic about blood is funny because it's simultaneously correct (periods are normal) and contextually absurd. The 'Dur' frames Abbi's concern as ignorant rather than reasonable. Good character inversion.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'I can't believe that diseased little rat chewed through my favorite period panties.' Ilana: 'Panty-eating rat perv.'
jokes.setup: The rat has caused property damage.
jokes.punchline: 'Panty-eating rat perv' — Ilana assigns sexual motivation to the rat, immediately anthropomorphizing pest behavior as deviance.
jokes.explanation: 'Panty-eating rat perv' is a great three-word unit — it's ridiculous, specific, and adds moral accusation to rodent behavior. The concept of a rat as a pervert is Ilana's unique filter on the world. High economy.

jokes.text: Abbi asks Ilana if she can be her 'Head Chef' for the party — delivered as if asking to be best man at a wedding.
jokes.setup: Abbi has wanted to ask this for a while and approaches it with extreme reverence.
jokes.punchline: The elaborate, emotional preamble ('don't feel any pressure,' 'it would mean the world to me') for what amounts to a minor party cooking role.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the tonal mismatch — massive emotional vulnerability for a request that has no stakes. Abbi treating 'Head Chef' as a sacred title she's been working up the courage to request is both pathetic and endearing. Works as friendship comedy about how seriously they take their micro-world.

jokes.text: Abbi quotes 'With great power comes great responsibility' and attributes it to Teddy Roosevelt. Ilana corrects: 'Spider-Man.' Abbi: 'Even better.'
jokes.setup: Ilana grants Abbi the Head Chef role. Abbi invokes a famous quote.
jokes.punchline: Misattributing Spider-Man to Teddy Roosevelt, then immediately accepting the Spider-Man correction and upgrading it — 'Even better' lands perfectly.
jokes.explanation: Three-beat structure executed perfectly: wrong attribution → correction → Abbi's sincere upgrade ('Even better'). The 'Even better' is the punch — she's not embarrassed, she genuinely prefers the Spider-Man provenance. Character-specific and economical. Among the stronger jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'I don't know! It was like I hated a part of myself. Now I only hate my cankles.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is processing her newfound self-acceptance after coming out.
jokes.punchline: The pivot from deep self-acceptance journey to 'Now I only hate my cankles' — she's replaced one self-hatred with a much more manageable one.
jokes.explanation: This is the best standalone joke in the first half of the episode. The structure is perfect: emotional sincerity about a real coming-out journey → immediate comic deflation with one of the most mundane possible body grievances ('cankles'). The contrast between the grandeur of queer self-acceptance and petty cankle-hatred is both funny and humanizing. 'Only' is the key word — implying this is genuine progress. Highly quotable, economical, character-specific. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: After Ilana says she needs to make out with someone, Abbi offers herself, then Ilana specifies 'someone who's attracted to women.' Then: 'Abbi, just so you know, every guy here's gonna be, like, a four-plus on the Kinsey scale.' / 'Gay.' / 'I know, that's why this gal joined Tinder.'
jokes.setup: Ilana wants a hookup at her Soulstice party with a gay-friendly crowd.
jokes.punchline: Kinsey scale being translated immediately to 'Gay' by Abbi, then Ilana's Tinder solution as a work-around to a gay party.
jokes.explanation: Abbi's 'Gay.' as a single-word translation of the Kinsey scale is the best beat here — perfect comic economy. The sequence works as a rapid-fire exchange but doesn't reach above competent.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'You must be très horny.' Abbi: 'I'm not Trey anything, Ilana... what?' Ilana: 'Okay, I won't show off my French, Abbi.'
jokes.setup: Ilana uses French to comment on Abbi's Tinder motivation.
jokes.punchline: Abbi mishears 'très' as the name 'Trey,' and Ilana responds by backing down as if she was being insufferably showy with her French skills.
jokes.explanation: The misunderstanding is good, but Ilana's response is the real joke — she retreats from her French as if Abbi is correct to be annoyed, treating a one-word French synonym as 'showing off.' Her social self-awareness is backwards: she's apologizing for a non-offense as if it's pretension. Also plants 'Trey' as a concept that pays off in the final scene.

jokes.text: Ilana mentions she gave Tinder dates this address 'so if they come back to chop you up, you're not even here.' Abbi: 'Yeah... Wait, what?'
jokes.setup: Ilana explains her Tinder safety strategy.
jokes.punchline: She gave out Abbi's address as a safety buffer — so murders happen at Abbi's, not hers. Abbi's delayed 'Wait, what?' is the reaction beat.
jokes.explanation: Ilana's logic is internally consistent and utterly monstrous — she has solved the Tinder-stranger-murder problem by simply exporting the murder risk to Abbi. The delayed 'Wait, what?' from Abbi is perfectly timed. Dark, specific to their friendship dynamic, and reveals the power imbalance in the relationship that makes Broad City work.

jokes.text: Ilana accidentally compliments a partygoer's 'money' instead of 'outfit': 'Love your money... outfit.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is playing hostess at the door, collecting cover charges.
jokes.punchline: Her brain is so focused on money that she starts saying 'money' instead of 'outfit' mid-compliment.
jokes.explanation: The slip-and-self-correct format works because the initial 'money' reveal is enough — you don't need the 'outfit' correction, but it being there shows Ilana catching herself and choosing to keep the sentence alive. Small, sharp, in-character.

jokes.text: Abbi is getting Tinder matches at the door: 'Tinder is so fucking easy, dude.' / 'I am rich in men. And those men are gonna make me rich... in money.' Then she sings '♪ I rich ♪'
jokes.setup: Abbi discovers how easy Tinder matching is.
jokes.punchline: 'I am rich in men. And those men are gonna make me rich... in money.' — the grammatically awkward turn to 'rich in money' plus the spontaneous song.
jokes.explanation: 'Rich in men... rich in money' works as a broken attempt at wit — she's reaching for something profound and lands on something clunky but earnest. The spontaneous 'I rich' song is a great cap that shows Abbi's joy even when her wordsmithing fails.

jokes.text: 'I am rich in men. And those men are gonna make me rich... in money.' followed by Abbi singing '♪ I rich, I rich ♪'
jokes.setup: Abbi is getting Tinder matches rapidly and connecting it to the cover charge revenue scheme
jokes.punchline: The deliberate clarification 'rich... in money' (not love/men) followed by her spontaneously singing about being rich is a delightful absurdist character beat
jokes.explanation: The clarification structure ('rich... in money') is a classic comedic move and it lands. The spontaneous '♪ I rich ♪' song is a great button that feels organic to these characters.

jokes.text: Rat sighting mid-party: 'Rat Bastard!'
jokes.setup: The rat was supposedly dealt with
jokes.punchline: The return of 'Rat Bastard!' as Ilana's go-to rat expletive — this is now a callback to the opening and establishes the running gag
jokes.explanation: The callback earns its repeat — by now 'Rat Bastard' has become Ilana's signature rat-specific curse and the return deepens the running gag.

jokes.text: Ilana tells Lincoln they need to distract guests from the rat: 'I need you to create a dish so good, so élégante, that the party is distracted and I can catch this fat-ass rat-ass myself.'
jokes.setup: They spot the rat at the party and need to manage the situation.
jokes.punchline: 'Fat-ass rat-ass' as a compound insult for the rat — internally rhyming, escalating, and specific to Ilana's vernacular.
jokes.explanation: 'Fat-ass rat-ass' is a nice piece of Ilana word-music — the double 'ass' rhyme gives it bounce. The whole assignment of Food Network-level cooking as a rat distraction is absurd problem-solving that's very on-brand.

jokes.text: Lincoln: 'This restaurant would definitely rate a "C" on the New York Sanitation Department scale.' Then upgrades to 'Actually, it would be a grade "D" 'cause of that moth larvae all over the ceiling.' Ilana: 'Yeah, I can't find the source.'
jokes.setup: Ilana pitches the cooking challenge as Food Network-level.
jokes.punchline: Lincoln's clinical assessment of health code violations escalates from C to D mid-sentence because he notices moth larvae. Ilana's breezy 'I can't find the source' treats this as a minor inconvenience.
jokes.explanation: Lincoln's self-correction mid-assessment (C → D) because he discovers new violations in real-time is a strong comic structure. The escalation is built into the single exchange. Ilana's 'I can't find the source' as a non-response response — treating moth larvae as a chronic minor mystery — is a great capper.

jokes.text: Tadd 26 arrives. Ilana: 'This is a really good photo of you.' Beat. 'It's almost too good, though.'
jokes.setup: Abbi's Tinder date arrives. Ilana greets him.
jokes.punchline: Ilana starts with a compliment then immediately and audibly walks it back — 'almost too good' implying his actual appearance is a disappointment relative to his profile.
jokes.explanation: 'Almost too good, though' is a masterpiece of diplomatic insult — the pause before it lets the compliment land, then the qualifier demolishes it while maintaining plausible deniability. Very Ilana: too honest to be polite but not honest enough to just say it. Economical and character-specific.

jokes.text: Party guest announces: 'Oh, my God, rat! Pack!' Then explains the rat had 'Fred Sinatra' in its crew and 'they would sing a cappella and watch each other fuck.'
jokes.setup: Rat is spotted at the party.
jokes.punchline: 'Fred Sinatra' is the rat's name. The Rat Pack is rat-themed — they sing a cappella and watch each other have sex. The sexual detail escalates the absurdity.
jokes.explanation: 'Fred Sinatra' is good — the Rat Pack joke being literalized into an actual rat gang is the episode title payoff. Adding 'they watch each other fuck' as the Rat Pack's defining activity is an excellent dark escalation of the Brat Pack/Rat Pack reputation for hedonism. Rewatch bonus for the episode title connection.

jokes.text: Lincoln, clearly high, says 'If I ever get my own food truck, I'm gonna call it "Lincoln's Center."' and acts like it just came to him.
jokes.setup: Lincoln is cooking while high.
jokes.punchline: 'Lincoln's Center' as a pun on Lincoln Center — the high-minded cultural institution versus a food truck. The idea clearly delights him in this moment.
jokes.explanation: Good pun, but what makes it funny is the implication that Lincoln is stoned enough that he thinks this is an original revelation. The delivery — as if this is the best idea he's ever had — is the comedy. Ilana's 'That is genius!' playing into his state makes it a brief shared delusion.

jokes.text: Lincoln checking his phone: 'What... No one. What? What? I didn't get a text from anybody.'
jokes.setup: Lincoln picks up his phone in a way that suggests he's expecting something
jokes.punchline: He preemptively and defensively announces he received no texts — the multiple 'What?'s suggesting he expected we were watching/asking
jokes.explanation: A small but sweet character beat for Lincoln — his defensiveness suggests social anxiety or loneliness that makes him sympathetic and funny.

jokes.text: Jaimé reveals he's been holding something back 'for a very long time' then delivers: 'Your mustache is terrible. It makes you look like you... like touch the little children.'
jokes.setup: Jaimé, emboldened by his coming-out journey and Steven's parallel transformation, decides to speak his truth.
jokes.punchline: The long wind-up for what amounts to a vicious mustache critique — specifically, that the mustache implies pedophilia.
jokes.explanation: The setup is doing real work — the 'very long time' build, the solemn new-honesty framing — and the punchline is utterly disproportionate to the gravitas. 'It makes you look like you... like touch the little children' with the hesitation before the accusation is perfectly structured. Jaimé following up his parent's olive branch with telling his roommate he looks like a pedophile is great thematic parallelism. High impact — this gets a big room reaction.

jokes.text: After the mustache speech, Jaimé says 'That felt good.' — a small beat of catharsis.
jokes.setup: Jaimé has just told his roommate his mustache makes him look like a child molester.
jokes.punchline: 'That felt good' — the minimal response that mirrors coming-out language applied to a petty insult, suggesting Jaimé is treating this as a parallel liberation.
jokes.explanation: Three words doing enormous thematic work. Jaimé has structured vicious appearance-shaming as an act of personal authenticity, mirroring the episode's self-acceptance theme with perfect comic irony. 'That felt good' borrows the emotional language of the coming-out narrative and applies it to cruelty. Economy score of 9.5 is fully justified.

jokes.text: Costume change mid-episode: 'Whoa, costume change?' — 'Yeah, I'm quirky.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has changed outfits apparently just for fun.
jokes.punchline: 'Yeah, I'm quirky' as total self-aware self-branding — the character acknowledging their own sitcom-character trait.
jokes.explanation: Quick, efficient meta-beat. 'I'm quirky' as self-diagnosis is funny because it's both accurate and insufficient. Works as a scene transition joke.

jokes.text: A Tinder date turns out to be much older than his profile photo. Ilana: 'You thought a young Denzel Washington was gonna show up for your Tinder date?'
jokes.setup: Abbi's Tinder date arrives and he's used an old photo.
jokes.punchline: The specific comparison to 'a young Denzel Washington' — Ilana's racial aesthetic preferences firing again, now as a criticism of Abbi's naivety.
jokes.explanation: The callback to Ilana's 'Is he black?' establishes this as a running character note. 'Young Denzel Washington' is a brilliant specific choice — not just 'a handsome Black man' but THE aspirational peak-era Denzel. Ilana's framing of the situation as Abbi failing to attract young Denzel is both absurd and completely in character. High impact — the specificity makes the room laugh.

jokes.text: 'You thought a young Denzel Washington was gonna show up for your Tinder date?'
jokes.setup: Abbi has clearly shown up for a Tinder date expecting someone much younger/more attractive
jokes.punchline: Ilana's rhetorical question — 'You thought a young Denzel Washington was gonna show up?' — both mocks Abbi's naivety and reveals the man's profile photo was apparently Denzel Washington
jokes.explanation: A great line — the implication that this old man's Tinder photo was literally young Denzel Washington is a specific and absurd punchline. Ilana's delivery ('You thought a young Denzel Washington...') is the kind of confident absurdist specificity this show excels at.

jokes.text: Abbi doesn't know you can swipe left on Tinder: 'Wait, what do you mean swipe left?' — implying she has been meeting up with every single match.
jokes.setup: Abbi has been proudly collecting Tinder matches and meets.
jokes.punchline: She didn't know about swipe left — meaning she's been accepting every single match and meeting up with all of them, including this one.
jokes.explanation: Strong retroactive recontextualization joke — we assumed Abbi was successfully matching, but it turns out she was matching with EVERYONE because she didn't know selection existed. This reframes her entire Tinder arc. The 'swipe left' reveal simultaneously explains the parade of guests and comments on Abbi's earnest, indiscriminate approach to connection. Great structural callback payoff.

jokes.text: Ilana needs a cat to catch the rat and has 'borrowed' the bodega cat: 'Jellicle Cat, I need you to find and kill this ballsack, Rat Bastard.'
jokes.setup: Ilana needs backup to catch the rat.
jokes.punchline: She has stolen a bodega cat and is addressing it like a hitman using full rat-epithet ('ballsack, Rat Bastard').
jokes.explanation: 'Ballsack, Rat Bastard' as the rat's full proper name is a great detail — like the rat has earned a formal title through continued offense. Borrowing the bodega cat without permission is quintessential Ilana problem-solving. The 'Jellicle' reference is a nice absurdist touch.

jokes.text: On the borrowed cat's gender: 'Or her. Hm, what you? Well, I kinda hope it's a guy because there's literally zero good guys here.'
jokes.setup: Ilana and Abbi discuss the cat's gender.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's desire for male energy is so desperate she's projecting onto the cat — and the 'zero good guys here' is a perfect encapsulation of the party's demographic problem.
jokes.explanation: The pivot from 'what gender is the cat' to 'I hope it's a male because I need men in my life right now' is a charming non-sequitur. Ilana's search for male attention extending to cat-gender-preference is gentle absurdism.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'I need to meet, like, real men in real life that I don't work with.' Abbi corrects herself: 'That I don't, like, work it with.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is expressing her romantic frustrations.
jokes.punchline: She accidentally says 'work with' (colleagues) then corrects to 'work it with' (sexual) — and insists 'That's what I meant to say.'
jokes.explanation: Small but genuine moment — the slip-and-overcorrect format works, and 'That's what I meant to say' is a good defensive move that makes it worse. Light wordplay.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Come on, Jellicle Cat, scheme, make some fucking trouble. Like, Tom and Jerry.' Then immediately: 'Oh, my God, the media, reducing even cats to a stereotype. See, I have to remember, I am the problem.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is coaching the cat to chase the rat.
jokes.punchline: She references Tom and Jerry, then immediately self-cancels — recognizing the cat-and-mouse binary as a media-driven stereotype, and confessing she has internalized it.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. It works on multiple levels: (1) The absurdity of analyzing cat media representation while trying to deploy a cat as a murder weapon. (2) 'I am the problem' as a genuine social justice admission applied to cartoon cat stereotyping. (3) The self-awareness being both correct (Tom and Jerry IS a single-story cartoon stereotype) and hilariously misplaced in context. This is peak Broad City — politically engaged comedy that recognizes its own absurdity in real time. Justifies 8+ on originality, structure, and character integration.

jokes.text: 'I am crushin' it right now. I'm definitely gonna be the Chop'd Champion tonight.'
jokes.setup: Lincoln is cooking food for an apartment party with a rat problem
jokes.punchline: Lincoln frames his apartment party cooking as a Chopped competition and declares himself the champion — the grandiosity applied to the circumstances is the joke
jokes.explanation: Good Lincoln character beat — his earnest professional pride in a deeply unprofessional context. Functional.

jokes.text: Lincoln after the rat runs over his foot: 'You know the band Ratatat? Let's listen to thatatat, mm-hmm.'
jokes.setup: The rat has just run over Lincoln's foot mid-cooking.
jokes.punchline: 'Thatatat' — extending the Ratatat wordplay into 'that' + 'tat' as a deflection from the horror of a rat foot contact.
jokes.explanation: Lincoln's stoned calm in the face of rat contact — pivoting immediately to a music recommendation with a freshly coined portmanteau — is excellent character work. 'Thatatat' as a nonce word is genuinely inventive. The 'mm-hmm' tail makes it funnier — he's committed to this as an actual suggestion.

jokes.text: Delivery man arrives: he dropped the basket at the wrong address because 'I keep messing up because I have dyslexia. Please, this is an SOS.'
jokes.setup: A stranger shows up claiming the basket was delivered to the wrong address.
jokes.punchline: Citing dyslexia as the reason for the number-reversal error — the vulnerability and urgency of 'this is an SOS' from a delivery man over a gift basket.
jokes.explanation: Functional plot joke — the dyslexia explanation is both plausible and comic. 'This is an SOS' is the best beat, treating gift basket recovery as an emergency. Serviceable but not inventive.

jokes.text: Abbi and Ilana stall the delivery man: 'We really wanna get in there, but we used our willpower.' — Ilana chimes in: 'No, no, we just made out.'
jokes.setup: They're trying to claim the basket is untouched while having clearly raided it.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's diplomatic lie about 'willpower' is immediately torpedoed by Ilana's honest '...we just made out' — confessing they made out instead of eating the basket, which somehow sounds worse.
jokes.explanation: Perfect two-part structure: Abbi covers → Ilana blows cover. But what makes it exceptional is that the truth (made out, didn't eat the basket) is presented as MORE strange than the lie. 'We just made out' is said with complete calm, as if this is reassuring information. Their friendship dynamic — Abbi trying to social-navigate while Ilana subverts it with truth-bombs — is perfectly captured in two lines. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Delivery man notices the basket is now in a shower curtain: 'Is that a shower curtain?'
jokes.setup: They've reassembled the raided basket in whatever was available.
jokes.punchline: The basket is so visibly degraded that even the recipient notices the improvised wrapping immediately.
jokes.explanation: Visual punchline that works through implication — we don't need to see the shower curtain basket; the question alone tells us everything. Economy score is high because the joke is entirely in four words.

jokes.text: Steven reveals he shaved with 'the pubey girl razor from the bathroom' — Jaimé's reaction: 'You look incredible.' / 'I was completely right. And the whole pedophile mustache was so distracting.'
jokes.setup: Jaimé told Steven his mustache made him look like a pedophile. Steven has now shaved.
jokes.punchline: The callback confirms Jaimé was right, but 'the pubey girl razor from the bathroom' adds a disgusting texture detail, and Jaimé doubling down on 'the pedophile mustache' terminology — now as a proper name for what it was.
jokes.explanation: Good callback — the mustache joke pays off. 'Pubey girl razor' is a gross detail that works as throwaway texture. 'The pedophile mustache' being used as a proper noun is a small but nice escalation of the original joke.

jokes.text: Others react to Steven and Jaimé's near-romance with 'Ew, dude!' — someone yells 'Homophobe!' and 'You hate the gays, man?' — But the reaction is actually to a mess, not the gay moment.
jokes.setup: The crowd sees Jaimé and Steven having a sweet moment and someone reacts negatively.
jokes.punchline: The 'Homophobe!' accusation is a misread — they're reacting to actual physical mess, not the gay moment. Anti-homophobia policing applied to the wrong stimulus.
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection — the audience's expectation (homophobia) is inverted (the 'ew' is about mess). The party crowd's instant 'Homophobe!' reflex also works as a comment on performative allyship — they're quick to condemn before getting the facts. Structural misdirection is clean.

jokes.text: Abbi asks the UPS/delivery man a personal question: 'You ever taken a sharp turn in one of those trucks without the doors on it, and then, you just fall out?' He says: 'All the time.' She immediately: 'Quick follow-up. Do you wanna make out right now?' — 'Yeah.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is in the kitchen with the delivery man, trying to stall.
jokes.punchline: The truck-falling-out question is so specific and odd it sounds like a fetish — then she immediately leverages his 'All the time' as the basis for a make-out proposition. The immediate 'Yeah' makes it work.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The truck question is beautifully weird — the specificity ('sharp turn,' 'without the doors') makes it feel like Abbi has a very particular thing for delivery men who've survived falls. His 'All the time' answer is unexpectedly enthusiastic, and her 'Quick follow-up' phrasing — treating the courtship like an interview — leads perfectly into the make-out proposal. The entire exchange has an improv-perfected quality: each beat tops the last. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'Everybody love cheese balls.' / 'I know, they're, like, too good.' / 'This is actual food.'
jokes.setup: Lincoln is presenting elevated party food in a trash-turned-treasure scenario
jokes.punchline: The conversation about cheese balls as genuine food revelation — 'This is actual food' delivered as a sincere compliment — deflates the Chopped pretension
jokes.explanation: Functional small joke — 'This is actual food' as a compliment sets a low bar, and the cheese ball enthusiasm is mildly funny.

jokes.text: Lincoln, high, tastes his food: 'Motherfuck, that's good.' / 'I am crushin' it right now.' / 'I'm definitely gonna be the Chop'd Champion tonight.'
jokes.setup: Lincoln has been cooking under pressure while clearly very high.
jokes.punchline: 'Chop'd' — a 'Chopped' parody — being the benchmark of his ambition. 'I am crushin' it right now' as stoned self-evaluation.
jokes.explanation: Pays off the 'Food Network-level' setup. 'Chop'd' as a knockoff show title is a small but nice touch — not Chopped, but Chop'd. Lincoln's high confidence is consistent character work but the beats are mild.

jokes.text: The Larry and David misunderstanding resolves: Ilana assumed the basket was from a gay couple. Corrected: 'Larry and David are brothers.' Ilana: 'Ew.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has been celebrating this gift basket as representing two men in a romantic relationship, planning to 'spread the seed of their gesture' at a party in their honor.
jokes.punchline: They're brothers. Ilana's 'Ew' reacts to the incest implication of how she's been framing their relationship (explosive sexual and romantic) — and also to the revelation that her parents' olive branch was actually from a delivery company.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best structural joke — a long-burn misunderstanding paying off on two levels simultaneously. (1) The Larry David reference finally resolves as a comedy franchise callback. (2) Ilana has been framing this as a romantic gay couple and called their relationship 'explosive sexual and romantic' — which retroactively becomes incest. 'Ew' does everything. The rewatch value is very high — the entire basket subplot is recontextualized. Perfect economy (single word punchline), earned across the whole episode, structurally 9/10. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Ilana chases the rat to the bathroom — it turns out to be a mother rat with babies. Ilana: 'She's a single gal just looking to raise her kids in a safe home. She's me!'
jokes.setup: The rat has been the villain of the episode.
jokes.punchline: Sudden identification with the rat as a fellow single female — 'She's me!' — a complete reversal of the episode's central conflict.
jokes.explanation: The thematic pivot of the episode — Ilana's empathy extending to the mother rat mirrors the episode's through-line about acceptance and identity. 'She's me!' is earned by the whole episode's energy around self-discovery. The rat is a single mother in NYC, just like the show's protagonists in spirit. Strong character integration: only Ilana would make this leap and mean it. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'That's what she said.' / 'That's something Trey would say.' / 'Oh my God, totally.'
jokes.setup: Another 'that's what she said' moment — now retroactively associated with the closet-drinking guy who made the same joke.
jokes.punchline: Ilana and Abbi now have a shared reference for TWSS jokes as 'something Trey would say' — they've named the type.
jokes.explanation: Excellent triple callback — the 'That's what she said' meta joke from earlier, plus the 'Trey' name from the très/Trey confusion. They've now retroactively named the guy 'Trey' and assigned the TWSS to him as his defining trait. This is elegant running gag construction: the episode created 'Trey' as a concept, and this is the payoff. Rewatch bonus for catching the connection.

jokes.text: Ilana sends the delivery man away with instructions to claim the basket was stolen: 'It's been fun. We're a great team.' He agrees enthusiastically.
jokes.setup: They need to get rid of the delivery man who wants the basket back.
jokes.punchline: Ilana has made a co-conspirator of a stranger in about ten minutes and frames a fraud scheme as teamwork. His agreement treats this as a genuine partnership.
jokes.explanation: Functional resolution comedy — Ilana's confident fraud instructions delivered as team-building. 'We're a great team' for a fraud they've known each other for 15 minutes is nice, but doesn't fully land as a big joke.

jokes.text: 'May God, She bless us. Every one.'
jokes.setup: The delivery situation has resolved and Ilana offers a benediction
jokes.punchline: Ilana's Dickens reference ('God bless us, every one') is upgraded with a feminist correction — 'God, She' — delivered as totally natural and sincere
jokes.explanation: The gendered God correction inserted into a Tiny Tim quote is a perfect Ilana move — political worldview instinctively applied to a classic line. Character integration is very high.

jokes.text: Jaimé, on new honesty path, tells Lincoln: 'I would kind of like to use our oven for cooking. Will you take your clothes out, please?'
jokes.setup: Jaimé is newly emboldened to speak his truth.
jokes.punchline: Lincoln stores his clothes in the oven — a NYC-poverty-appropriate detail that has apparently been a long-standing grievance Jaimé never voiced.
jokes.explanation: The oven-as-closet is a New York City poverty-specific joke that works because it's true — people use ovens for storage. Lincoln's acceptance ('Absolutely, I hear that') treating this as a reasonable and new request implies he genuinely doesn't see the problem. Good character specificity for both characters.

jokes.text: Jaimé: 'Ilana, I don't want to do your bacne anymore.' Ilana: 'What?' / Jaimé: 'I'm sorry, it's just not my thing. You should see a dermatologist.' Ilana: 'Would you mind just doing one last one, the middle... massive middle back.'
jokes.setup: Jaimé is using his new honesty to reveal long-suppressed grievances.
jokes.punchline: He's been popping Ilana's back acne as a favor for an unspecified period, never wanting to. Ilana's immediate negotiation — 'just one last one, the massive middle back' — is both horrifying and classic.
jokes.explanation: Bacne-popping as a long-term friendship service — the reveal that this has been an ongoing thing Jaimé hates is perfect cringe comedy. But the real joke is Ilana's response: she doesn't dispute his right to stop, she just negotiates for 'the massive middle back' as a last request, which is so specific it implies she knows exactly which pore she needs done. The specificity ('massive middle back') is what elevates this from cringe to great cringe. High character integration: this could only happen in this specific friendship. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Jaimé's cultural appropriation speech: 'There is something you do that I see a lot of white people do... you know those earrings that say "Latina"? They look beautiful on you. But you not Latina anymore.'
jokes.setup: Jaimé is using his new honesty to address cultural appropriation by Ilana.
jokes.punchline: 'You not Latina anymore' — as if she once was, or as if being Latina is something you can lose through identity reassignment. The logic is both correct (she shouldn't wear them) and absurd (she was never Latina).
jokes.explanation: The earrings compliment before the takedown ('They look beautiful on you. But...') is good structural cruelty. 'You not Latina anymore' implies there was a period when she was, which is absurd but also works because Ilana has clearly been performing a cultural identity. Good social commentary embedded in character comedy.

jokes.text: Jaimé explains cultural appropriation and concludes: 'it's almost like you are the colonists.'
jokes.setup: Jaimé has been building to a cultural appropriation argument.
jokes.punchline: 'It's almost like you are the colonists' — escalating from 'cultural appropriation' all the way to 'you personally are a colonialist force.'
jokes.explanation: The logic is airtight and the escalation is earned — he walks through the argument properly before landing 'you are the colonists.' The 'almost like' qualifier is perfect: it's a hedged accusation that lands harder for being softened. Broad City being explicit about colonial power structures through Jaimé's character is both genuinely engaged and funny. Ilana's 'Ah!' of recognition is a great silent beat.

jokes.text: Jaimé: 'Also, mi amor, can you please get off Grindr? You have a Grindr profile and it's kind of our thing, you know?' Lincoln: 'I just wanna see penises within a mile of me.' Jaimé: 'I'm so sorry, I've gone too far. You can see all the penises you want. Teach me.'
jokes.setup: Jaimé is on a roll of honest revelations.
jokes.punchline: Lincoln's totally sincere 'I just wanna see penises within a mile of me' as a Grindr defense — and Jaimé's immediate capitulation and request to be taught.
jokes.explanation: Among the episode's best jokes. 'I just wanna see penises within a mile of me' is a perfect line — it's an entirely reasonable explanation for Grindr presented with complete sincerity, and it's immediately winning. Jaimé's 'I'm so sorry, I've gone too far' treating this as a boundary he should not have crossed, then 'Teach me' — surrendering the position entirely and asking for instruction on penis-proximity apps — is a perfect escalating close. Justifies 8+. 'Teach me' as the final word of the exchange is brilliant economy.

jokes.text: Final beat: 'All the penises you want.' / 'Teach me.' / 'All the penises you want.' / 'Teach me.' / 'Penis.'
jokes.setup: Jaimé has conceded the Grindr territory completely.
jokes.punchline: The exchange escalates into a call-and-response that ends on a single, triumphant 'Penis.' — Lincoln reducing the entire thing to one word.
jokes.explanation: The final 'Penis.' as a standalone episode-closing declaration is perfect comedy construction. The call-and-response builds rhythm, and the single-word closer has the quality of a great button. It's earned by the whole preceding exchange and delivers with maximum economy. Lincoln teaching through repetition, then just saying 'Penis' as if that's the complete lesson — excellent. Justifies 8+.
